text: The video shows a well-healed scar in the patient's right iliac fossa from a previous kidney transplantation. A single port laparoscopic method is employed, with the operative access site marked inferior and to the right of the umbilicus, which will also serve as the site for the end ileostomy.

The extraperitoneal transplanted kidney is visible in the right iliac fossa, with the transplanted ureter extending into the pelvis. The cecum is slightly displaced anteriorly, elevated due to the kidney's placement, with no intraperitoneal adhesions observed.
The surgeon identifies the appendix, visually distinguishing it from surrounding structures within the surgical field.

The surgeon removes the small intestine from the pelvis, aided by positioning the patient head down and left side up, which provides clear exposure of the distal sigmoid colon and proximal rectum. Dissection begins distally at the rectal sigmoid, followed by staple transection of the distal sigmoid, and continues proximally along a close pericolic plane.
The camera focuses on the transplanted ureter and renal artery, both clearly visible as they travel into the pelvis.
The surgeon begins the dissection of the distal sigmoid colon from its mesocolon using a Medtronic ligature device, achieving excellent hemostatic control and precise tissue separation.
The surgeon opens a window through the mesocolon after clean dissection of the distal colon, preparing for a stapled distal transection using a Medtronic ENDO-GIA-SIXTY device.

The surgeon positions the stapler within a separate finger of the glove port to transect the fully dissected distal colon. Care is taken to accurately place the stapler amidst the cluster of instruments, ensuring no pelvic side wall structures are caught during the single stapler fire.

The surgeon detaches the sigmoid colon from the rectum and begins dissecting the sigmoid mesocolon from the sigmoid colon, extending proximally. The stepwise dissection is performed with careful traction by an assisting instrument, providing counterpoint to the ligature device operated by the primary surgeon.

The surgeon moves the colon medially, revealing the left ureter running along the pelvic wall towards the bottom of the screen. The dissection remains in a close pericolic plane to avoid the retroperitoneal ureter and preserve the gonadal vessels.
The surgeon uses a ligature device to coagulate a bleeding mesocolic vessel, achieving hemostatic control within the surgical field.

The surgeon approaches the descending colon, dissecting its lateral attachments using a lateral approach. A distracting instrument is frequently reapplied to maintain appropriate tension on the tissue being dissected.

The surgeon continues dissecting the descending colon, tilting the patient into a head-up, left-side-up position to facilitate bringing the splenic flexure into view. A combination of medial and lateral dissection is performed, and the omentum is removed from both its anterior and posterior attachments to the transverse colon.

The surgeon mobilizes the transverse colon by dissecting it from the omentum and its mesocolon, entering the lesser sac while ensuring protection of the stomach and its vessels. The middle colic arteries are visible in this quadrant, requiring careful sealing.

The surgeon mobilizes the transverse colon along its distal two-thirds and proceeds to dissect the right colon, focusing on the cecum and ascending colon. The cecum, ileum, and appendix are elevated to facilitate dissection of the associated mesentery, revealing a clear plane between the right colon and the kidney transplant. The dissection continues cephaladilaterally towards the hepatic flexure in a stepwise manner.
The surgeon moves medially to dissect the mesocolon of the right colon, ensuring the duodenum is visible and preserved during the procedure.

The surgeon locates and removes the distal end of the colon through the single port access site. An extracorporeal separation of the ileum from the cecum is performed, after which the ileum is returned to the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon performs relaparoscopy to ensure hemostasis and wash out any pooled fluid or blood. The distal ileum is checked for orientation and withdrawn through the access site for the formation of an end ileostomy.
